For the first challenge, a new regularisation term is proposed to make relative sketch-photo feature-distances uniform across categories, enabling a single global margin parameter to work across all categories.

The proposed regularisation term aims to minimize the KL-divergence between every pair of distributions of relative distances for sketch-positive-negative hard-triplets in a category, training the model to make relative sketch-photo distances uniform across categories.

The clever patch shuffling technique involves shuffling equally divided patches of a sketch and its corresponding photo then ensuring a shuffled sketch is closer to a shuffled photo with the same permutation order, while training for permutation-invariance to improve structural correspondences.

Justifying design components: We evaluate our models, dropping one component at a time (Table 2) for both ZS-.
Both ZS-SBIR and FG-ZS-SBIR models are evaluated in the context mentioned, indicating a relationship in terms of evaluation and comparison.
The absence of f-Divergence affects FG-ZS-SBIR, leading to a severe impact on its class-discrimination ability, highlighting a critical relationship between the two terms.
Removing LPS (Patch-Shuffling) results in a significant decrease in FG-ZS-SBIR accuracy by 3.15%, underlining the importance of LPS in maintaining accuracy.

The absence of Lδ (f-Divergence) leads to a decrease in FG-ZS-SBIR accuracy by 3.75%, indicating the role of Lδ in preserving the structural correspondence and relative distances in sketch-photo relationships.
Using 2 × 2 patches instead of 3 × 3 for FG-ZS-SBIR results in a 1.2% gain in Acc.@1 (Sketchy), highlighting the impact of patch size on the model's accuracy.

CLIP text encoder from the method mentioned replaces Word2vec in obtaining category semantics, with CLIP text encoder leveraging large-scale image-text pairs for training compared to Word2vec trained on text-only data, showcasing a shift in methodology.
Using word2vec embeddings instead of CLIP drops performance by 4.57%/0.172 for ZS-SBIR/FG-ZS-SBIR on Sketchy, justifying the choice of CLIP's text encoder for capturing visual-semantic association.
Learning text prompts drops performance by 2.36%/0.078 in Sketchy for ZS-SBIR/ZS-SBIR, possibly due to the higher generalisation ability of handcrafted prompts rooted in natural language vocabulary.

To ensure the visual prompt has sufficient information capacity, the experiment is conducted with varying K values. The accuracy improves from 26.15%/0.675 at K = 1 to 28.68%/0.723 at K = 3 but saturates at K = 4, with optimal K being 3.
In this context, a high capacity prompt might lead to over-fitting of CLIP, resulting in lower zero-shot performance.
